name: camels-ind

env_axis.name: country
env_axis.shift_mechanism: monsoon-dominated tropical hydrology vs CAMELS-US temperate regime

facts: ~470 Indian catchments × multi-decade daily; schema-aligned with CAMELS-US per CAMELS family standard.
hypotheses: India introduces categorically new mechanisms (monsoon discharge regime) rather than a transitive 3-way structure (US→DK ≈ US→IND ∘ IND→DK), because tropical monsoon hydrology is not interpolable from temperate-zone pairs.
Monsoon-dominated hydrology is a mechanism shift relative to the temperate zone, requiring structural-profile extension beyond P=3.
intuitions: Cross-CAMELS analyses spanning US/DK/IND/CL/AUS/BR/CH/GB likely exist or are being assembled.
Transfer-learning work specific to Indian hydrology probably exists.
unknowns: Whether the CAMELS schema can adequately represent monsoon dynamics at daily resolution with standard forcings cannot be determined, since the schema-alignment that makes the pair tractable may lossily project monsoon signal.
Catchment partition is plausibly more problematic in non-temperate river networks, but this cannot be assessed from the data.
epistemic_state.boundary: The dataset inherits the CAMELS family's C=0 and adds tropical/monsoon hydrology as the third leg of the cross-country triple.

representational_commitments: Daily resolution may coarsen monsoon storm dynamics
Schema-aligned with US (potentially lossy for monsoon)
materialization_status: BLOCKED: Zenodo record 14005378 is access_right=restricted. Files list returns empty via public API. Mangukiya et al. 2025 ESSD vol 17 (doi:10.5194/essd-17-461-2025) published the paper but the data archive requires access request approval from authors (Nikunj Mangukiya, IIT Roorkee; nikk.mangukiya@gmail.com). Once approved, the 472-catchment hydrometeorological time series + 228-subset streamflow + 211 attributes can be downloaded.
